div.head:
3486.1 Robert Southey to [Unknown Correspondent], [26 May 1820] Endorsement: R Southey
MS: Special Collections Research Center, University of Kentucky Libraries. ALS; 3p.
Unpublished.
Dating note: Dating from contents – Southey left London for Cambridge on 27 May 1820.
div.p: I have a distinct recollection of you, as you may perceive by this token, that you made enquiry of me concerning the author of Count Julian,Count Julian: a Tragedy (1812), by Walter Savage Landor. The work was published anonymously. & were one of the few persons who properly appreciated his merits as a poet.

It would give me great pleasure to renew & improve my acquaintance with you. And I should also have been much gratified by an opportunity of becoming known to Sir William Curtis, Sir William Curtis, 1st Baronet (1752–1829; DNB), merchant, banker and prominent figure in the government of the City of London. He was one of the MPs for the City 1790–1818 and 1820–1826. Curtis was a consistent supporter of the government. At a celebration dinner on 6 April 1820, following his re-election as an MP for the City after a bitter struggle with his Whig opponents, he had denounced radical agitators and praised the Ministry. Curtis was also a supporter of George IV in his battle with his estranged wife, Queen Caroline (1768–1821; DNB). respecting as I do the course & the manliness of his political conduct. But I am too deeply engaged to allow of this. I have to go thro much business of many kinds, & do not expect to have any thing like a feeling of rest till I seat myself in the mail coach for Penrith, in the last week of June.
Whenever you may visit our part of the country again, remember that you need no introduction.

postscript.p: I am going to Cambridge tomorrow for the sake of seeing our friend Neville
